prompt:
I picked up the medal-board branch for the county games site after the original author rotated off, and g++ greets me with a 118-line wall of text. I've read error novels before but this one has everything: dependent scopes, 'near match' candidates, an instantiation stack three levels deep. Excerpts, faithfully copied:

    board.h: In function 'int medal_total(const Rows&)':
    board.h:52:10: error: need 'typename' before 'Rows::const_iterator' because 'Rows' is a dependent scope [-Wtemplate-body]
    board.h:52:50: error: 'it' was not declared in this scope; did you mean 'int'? [-Wtemplate-body]
    board.h:52:66: error: expected ')' before ';' token [-Wtemplate-body]

    fmt.h: In function 'std::string board_line(const F&, const T&)':
    fmt.h:18:16: error: expected 'template' keyword before dependent template name [-Werror=missing-template-keyword]
       18 |     return fmt.render<T>(row);

    test_medals.cpp: In function 'void test_table_order_counts_gold_first()':
    test_medals.cpp:28:16: error: no match for call to '(const MedalOrder) (ClubRow&, ClubRow&)'
      • candidate 1: 'bool MedalOrder::operator()(const ClubRow&, const ClubRow&)' (near match)
      • passing 'const MedalOrder*' as 'this' argument discards qualifiers

    board.h: In instantiation of 'std::size_t detail::lead_index(const std::vector<T>&, const Cmp&) [with T = ClubRow; Cmp = MedalOrder; std::size_t = long unsigned int]':
    board.h:32:62:   required from 'const T& Board<T, Cmp>::leader() const [with T = ClubRow; Cmp = MedalOrder]'
    test_medals.cpp:44:5:   required from here
    board.h:16:16: error: no match for call to '(const MedalOrder) (const __gnu_cxx::__alloc_traits<std::allocator<ClubRow>, ClubRow>::value_type&, const __gnu_cxx::__alloc_traits<std::allocator<ClubRow>, ClubRow>::value_type&)'

The part that spooks me is that 'no match for call' one: the candidate it prints takes exactly the arguments it says it can't match, so the actual complaint is hiding somewhere in the fine print, and the __gnu_cxx::__alloc_traits spelling of what is obviously just a ClubRow doesn't help. The ranking logic itself was reviewed and signed off — gold before silver before bronze, alphabetical on full ties, the ticker's dash format — and the tests pin all of it, so whatever's wrong should not require rewriting how anything ranks or renders.

Get `make test` compiling and green. test_medals.cpp, mintest.h and the Makefile are frozen — the fixes belong in the three headers.
notes: 5C4 item 4: template instantiation error novel. g++ 16.1.1, -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra -Werror. Baseline dump is 118 lines from three one-token causes, all firing in one pass (no chain reveals): (1) MedalOrder::operator() missing const — produces BOTH the direct 'discards qualifiers' near-match errors at the frozen test's `const MedalOrder order;` call sites (these pin the const fix: making Board's cmp_ member mutable or copying it non-const cannot green the tests) AND the catalog's instantiation-stack novel through Board::leader() const -> detail::lead_index -> cmp(...) with the ClubRow spelled as __gnu_cxx::__alloc_traits<...>::value_type; (2) missing `typename` before Rows::const_iterator in medal_total's for-init (block-scope declarations are NOT covered by C++20's P0634 typename relaxation — probed; gcc also cascades ''it' was not declared' plus a misleading-indentation error off it); (3) missing `.template` on fmt.render<T>(row) in board_line ('expected template keyword before dependent template name'). gcc 16 diagnoses (2) and (3) at template definition ([-Wtemplate-body]), so nothing hides behind instantiation. Differentiation from cpp-vatstats (5S4#6, missing typename paired with `>>` nesting in one header): this seed's center is the missing-const comparator novel and reading instantiation stacks, the typename site is a for-loop iterator declaration rather than an alias, and the disambiguator half of the pair is `.template`, which vatstats does not touch. Note std::stable_sort in ranked() copies the comparator and would accept a non-const operator() — leader()'s const-ref path and the test's const call sites are what make the const fix unavoidable. Reference fix is exactly three tokens: `const` on the call operator, `typename`, `template`.
